question: Define an order relation between two cardinal numbers. State and prove the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem.
expectedPoints: Definition: For cardinals |A| and |B|, |A| ≤ |B| if there exists an injective function from A into B.
Proof outline: Construct a bijection h: A -> B using the injections f: A -> B and g: B -> A by partitioning A and B into sequences and defining h appropriately on those sequences.

question: Explain why the relation ≤ on cardinal numbers is a partial order. Which property of a total order does it lack?
expectedPoints: Antisymmetry: |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |A| implies |A| = |B| by the Schröder-Bernstein Theorem.
Transitivity: |A| ≤ |B| and |B| ≤ |C| implies |A| ≤ |C| by composing injective functions.
It lacks totality: Without the Axiom of Choice, it is not provable that for any two sets A and B, either |A| ≤ |B| or |B| ≤ |A| holds.

question: State the definition of the order relation ≤ for cardinal numbers.
expectedPoints: For two sets A and B, the cardinal number of A, denoted |A|, is less than or equal to the cardinal number of B, denoted |B|.
This is defined if and only if there exists an injective function (one-to-one mapping) from the set A into the set B.
This definition is independent of the specific choice of sets A and B representing the cardinal numbers.
